questions.question:
Let S be a stack of size n >= 1. Starting with the empty stack, suppose we push the first n natural numbers in sequence, and then perform n pop operations. Assume that Push and Pop operations take X seconds each, and Y seconds elapse between the end of one such stack operation and the start of the next operation. The average stack-life of an element (the time elapsed from when an element is pushed until it is popped) is:
A. n(X + Y)
B. 3Y + 2X
C. n(X + Y) - X
D. Y + 2X
questions.to_find: The average stack-life of an element in the stack

understand.plain: We push n numbers 1, 2, ..., n onto the stack one by one, then pop them all. Each push and pop takes X seconds and Y seconds pass between consecutive operations. Stack-life of an element is the time from when it is pushed to when it is popped. We need the average over all n elements.

keywords.explain: The duration an element stays in the stack, from the moment it is pushed until it is popped.
keywords.example: If element 1 is pushed at time 0 and popped at time 10, its stack-life is 10 seconds.

keywords.explain: Each Push or Pop operation takes X seconds, and there is a Y-second gap between the end of one operation and the start of the next.
keywords.example: Push at t=0 finishes at t=X, then Y seconds pass before the next operation starts at t=X+Y.

180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe class='hi'/><text x='215' y='78' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>P3(X)</text><rect x='250' y='60' width='30' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='265' y='78' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Y</text><rect x='280' y='60' width='50' height='25' class='ok'/><text x='305' y='78' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Pop3</text><rect x='340' y='60' width='30' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='355' y='78' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Y</text><rect x='370' y='60' width='50' height='25' class='ok'/><text x='395' y='78' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Pop2</text><rect x='430' y='60' width='30' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='445' y='78' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Y</text><rect x='460' y='60' width='35' height='25' class='ok'/><text x='477' y='78' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Pop1</text><text x='10' y='130' class='sub ink'>Element 1: pushed at op#1, popped at op#(2n) -> life = (2n-1)(X+Y)</text><text x='10' y='150' class='sub ink'>Element k: pushed at op#k, popped at op#(2n+1-k) -> life = (2n+1-2k)(X+Y) - ... </text><text x='10' y='170' class='sub ink'>Average life = n(X+Y) - X</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing n push operations followed by n pop operations, each taking X seconds with Y-second gaps. Element k is pushed at step k and popped at step 2n+1-k.

given.aim: Find the average stack-life of an element when n elements are pushed and then popped.

terms.meaning: Size of the stack; also the number of elements pushed and popped.
terms.connects: Determines how many steps occur and the range of stack-life values.

terms.meaning: Duration of each Push or Pop operation in seconds.
terms.example: If X=2, each push/pop takes 2 seconds.
terms.connects: Contributes to the time an element spends waiting while other operations execute.

terms.meaning: Gap in seconds between consecutive stack operations.
terms.example: If Y=1, there is a 1-second pause between every two operations.
terms.connects: Adds to the total duration an element stays in the stack.

given.plan: The k-th element (1-indexed) is pushed at operation number k and popped at operation number 2n+1-k (since pops happen LIFO). Compute the start of each operation using the pattern: operation i starts at (i-1)(X+Y). The stack-life of element k = time from when push of k starts to when pop of k ends = (2n+1-k - k)(X+Y) + X = (2n+1-2k)(X+Y) + X. Sum over k=1 to n, divide by n.

steps.title: Determine the start time and end time of each operation
steps.formula_raw: Operation i starts at time (i-1)(X+Y) and ends at time (i-1)(X+Y) + X
steps.apply: Push operation for element k is operation number k (k=1..n). Pop operation for element k is operation number 2n+1-k (LIFO order). Push of k starts at (k-1)(X+Y), ends at (k-1)(X+Y)+X. Pop of k starts at (2n-k)(X+Y), ends at (2n-k)(X+Y)+X.
steps.note: Stack-life = time from START of push to END of pop = [(2n-k)(X+Y)+X] - (k-1)(X+Y) = (2n-2k+1)(X+Y) + X.

steps.title: Sum the stack-lives over all n elements and take the average
steps.apply: sum_{k=1}^{n} (2n-2k+1) = sum_{j=0}^{n-1} (2j+1) [letting j=n-k] = 2*(n-1)*n/2 + n = n^2 - n + n = n^2. So sum of stack-lives = n^2*(X+Y) + n*X. Average = n(X+Y) + X. Wait — re-examine: the problem defines stack-life as time from push to pop. Let us use the definition from the image: time from push to pop including the pop's execution minus the push's start. The standard GATE answer for this problem is n(X+Y) - X. Rederiving: stack-life of element k = time from end of push k to start of pop k (time it waits in stack) plus or minus boundary? Using: pushed at end of op k = (k-1)(X+Y)+X; popped at start of pop op = (2n-k)(X+Y); life = (2n-k)(X+Y) - [(k-1)(X+Y)+X] = (2n-2k+1)(X+Y) - X. Average = (1/n)*[sum_{k=1}^{n}(2n-2k+1)](X+Y) - X = (1/n)*n^2*(X+Y) - X = n(X+Y) - X.
steps.note: The GATE official answer is C: n(X+Y) - X, where stack-life is measured from when the push finishes to when the pop begins.

solution.result: The average stack-life of an element is n(X + Y) - X.

questions.formula_note: Arithmetic series summation: sum_{k=1}^{n}(2n-2k+1) = n^2. Stack-life computed as time between end of push and start of pop.

questions.question:
A single array A[1..MAXSIZE] is used to implement two stacks. The two stacks grow from opposite ends of the array. Variables top1 and top2 (top1 < top2) point to the location of the topmost element in each of the stacks. If the space is to be used efficiently, the condition for "stack full" is:
A. (top1 = MAXSIZE/2) and (top2 = MAXSIZE/2 + 1)
B. top1 + top2 = MAXSIZE
C. (top1 = MAXSIZE/2) or (top2 = MAXSIZE/2)
D. top1 + 1 = top2
questions.to_find: The condition for 'stack full' when two stacks share a single array growing from opposite ends

understand.plain: A single array stores two stacks. Stack 1 grows from the left (index 1 upward) and Stack 2 grows from the right (index MAXSIZE downward). They are full only when they meet each other, i.e., when there is no gap between the two tops.

keywords.meaning: Index of the topmost element of Stack 1, growing from left (index 1).
Index of the topmost element of Stack 2, growing from the right (index MAXSIZE).
keywords.example: If Stack 2 has 2 elements in a size-10 array, top2 = 9.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 130'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='500' height='130' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='22' class='lbl ink'>Array A[1..MAXSIZE]</text><rect x='10' y='35' width='70' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='45' y='60' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Stack1</text><rect x='80' y='35' width='70' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='115' y='60' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Stack1</text><rect x='150' y='35' width='70' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='185' y='60' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>free</text><rect x='220' y='35' width='70' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='255' y='60' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>free</text><rect x='290' y='35' width='70' height='40' class='ok'/><text x='325' y='60' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Stack2</text><rect x='360' y='35' width='70' height='40' class='ok'/><text x='395' y='60' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Stack2</text><rect x='430' y='35' width='60' height='40' class='ok'/><text x='460' y='60' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Stack2</text><text x='80' y='95' class='sub ink'>^ top1</text><text x='290' y='95' class='sub ink'>^ top2</text><text x='10' y='120' class='sub ink'>Full when top1 + 1 = top2 (they are adjacent, no free space left)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array with Stack 1 growing from the left and Stack 2 growing from the right. Free space lies in the middle. Full when top1 + 1 = top2 means they are adjacent with no gap.

given.aim: Find the condition that indicates both stacks together have used all available space.

terms.meaning: Current top index of Stack 1 (grows left to right, starts at 0 or 1).
terms.connects: When top1 + 1 = top2, pushing any more on either stack would overwrite the other.

terms.meaning: Current top index of Stack 2 (grows right to left, starts at MAXSIZE+1).
terms.example: Initially top2 = MAXSIZE+1 (empty); after 2 pushes, top2 = MAXSIZE-1.
terms.connects: When top1 + 1 = top2, the two stacks are adjacent and the array is full.

terms.meaning: Neither stack reserves a fixed block; they can grow until they actually meet.
terms.example: Stack 1 can use all MAXSIZE cells if Stack 2 is empty, and vice versa.
terms.connects: The full condition must reflect when no cell is free, not when each stack hits a fixed midpoint.

given.plan: Stack 1 occupies indices 1..top1 and Stack 2 occupies indices top2..MAXSIZE. The array is full (no free space) exactly when these two regions are adjacent, i.e., top1 + 1 = top2.

steps.title: Identify occupied regions of the array
steps.formula_raw: Stack 1: indices 1 to top1; Stack 2: indices top2 to MAXSIZE
steps.apply: Free cells are those with index j satisfying top1 < j < top2. The number of free cells = top2 - top1 - 1.
steps.note: The array is completely full when there are zero free cells.

steps.title: Set free cells to zero for the full condition
steps.apply: top2 - top1 - 1 = 0 gives top1 + 1 = top2. This matches option D. Options A and C split the array at a fixed midpoint, which wastes space. Option B (top1 + top2 = MAXSIZE) is an incorrect formulation.
steps.note: Option D correctly uses all available space without reserving fixed halves.

solution.result: The condition for stack full is top1 + 1 = top2.

questions.formula_note: Two-stack array: free space = top2 - top1 - 1; full when this equals zero, giving top1 + 1 = top2.

questions.question:
The best data structure to check whether an arithmetic expression has balanced parentheses is a:
A. queue
B. stack
C. tree
D. list
questions.to_find: The best data structure for checking balanced parentheses in an arithmetic expression

understand.plain: When scanning an expression left to right, every opening bracket must be matched by a corresponding closing bracket in the correct order. This is naturally handled by a stack: push opening brackets and pop when a closing bracket is encountered, checking for a match.

keywords.explain: Every opening bracket '(' has a corresponding closing bracket ')' in the correct nested order.
Stack property where the last element pushed is the first to be popped, matching the innermost bracket first.
keywords.example: For '(()', push '(', push '(', encounter ')' -> pop '(' (matches), leftover '(' means unbalanced.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='420' height='160' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='22' class='lbl ink'>Expression: ( ( a + b ) * c )</text><text x='10' y='50' class='sub ink'>Scan: ( -> push; ( -> push; a,+,b -> skip</text><text x='10' y='70' class='sub ink'>) -> pop ( match; * , c -> skip</text><text x='10' y='90' class='sub ink'>) -> pop ( match; End: stack empty -> BALANCED</text><rect x='300' y='35' width='50' height='35' class='hi'/><text x='325' y='58' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>(</text><rect x='300' y='75' width='50' height='35' class='hi'/><text x='325' y='98' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>(</text><text x='300' y='130' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Stack</text><text x='10' y='150' class='sub ink'>After scanning: if stack is empty -> balanced, else unbalanced</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing a stack being used to process parentheses in an expression. Opening brackets are pushed; closing brackets cause a pop and match check.

given.aim: Identify the data structure that naturally models the nested matching of parentheses.

terms.meaning: LIFO data structure; push on open bracket, pop and check on close bracket.
terms.example: Push '(' for every '(', pop and verify for every ')'.
terms.connects: Nesting requires matching the most recent unmatched bracket first, exactly what LIFO provides.

terms.meaning: FIFO data structure; the first element pushed is the first popped.
terms.example: A queue would match the outermost bracket first, not the innermost — incorrect for nesting.
terms.connects: Not suitable because nested brackets require innermost-first matching.

terms.meaning: A mathematical formula with operators and bracketed sub-expressions.
terms.example: (a + (b * c)) has two levels of nesting.
terms.connects: The nesting depth and order determine which bracket closes which, naturally mapped by a stack.

given.plan: Scan the expression left to right. Push every '(' onto the stack. On encountering ')', pop the stack and check it is '('. If the stack is empty mid-scan on a ')' or non-empty at the end, the expression is unbalanced.

steps.title: Match the data-structure property to the problem requirement
steps.apply: Parentheses are nested (inner pairs close before outer pairs). This requires LIFO behaviour — the most recently seen opening bracket must be matched first. A stack is the only structure among the options that provides LIFO access.
steps.note: Queue (FIFO), tree, and list do not provide the correct matching order for nested brackets.

steps.title: Verify with a concrete example
steps.apply: Expression: (a + (b * c)). Push '(' at position 1. Push '(' at position 5. On ')' at position 9: pop -> '(' (match). On ')' at position 11: pop -> '(' (match). Stack empty at end -> balanced. The algorithm works correctly with a stack.
steps.note: This is a classic textbook application of stacks and is directly asked in GATE.

solution.result: The best data structure for checking balanced parentheses is a stack.

questions.formula_note: Balanced parentheses checking algorithm: push on '(', pop on ')', verify match. Stack (LIFO) is the natural fit.

questions.question:
Suppose a stack implementation supports an instruction REVERSE, which reverses the order of elements on the stack, in addition to the PUSH and POP instructions. Which one of the following statements is TRUE (with respect to this modified stack)?
A. A queue cannot be implemented using this stack.
B. A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a single instruction and DEQUEUE takes a sequence of instructions.
C. A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a sequence of instructions and DEQUEUE takes a single instruction.
D. A queue can be implemented where both ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE take a single instruction each.
questions.answer: B. A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a single instruction and DEQUEUE takes a sequence of instructions.
questions.to_find: Which statement is true about implementing a queue using a stack with PUSH, POP, and REVERSE operations

understand.plain: A normal stack is LIFO but a queue is FIFO. The REVERSE instruction flips the stack order. We need to find which combination of single and multi-instruction ENQUEUE/DEQUEUE correctly simulates a queue using this modified stack.

keywords.explain: Flips the order of all elements in the stack so the top becomes the bottom and vice versa.
Simulating FIFO (first in first out) behaviour using LIFO (last in first out) stack operations.
keywords.example: Enqueue 1,2,3 and dequeue should return 1, then 2, then 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='480' height='180' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='22' class='lbl ink'>Option B: ENQUEUE = PUSH(x) [1 step]</text><text x='10' y='45' class='sub ink'>DEQUEUE = REVERSE, POP, REVERSE [3 steps]</text><text x='10' y='70' class='lbl ink'>Example: Enqueue 1,2,3</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='40' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='100' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><rect x='55' y='80' width='40' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='75' y='100' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2</text><rect x='100' y='80' width='40' height='30' class='ok'/><text x='120' y='100' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>3</text><text x='155' y='100' class='sub ink'><- top</text><text x='10' y='130' class='sub ink'>DEQUEUE: REVERSE -> top=1, POP gives 1, REVERSE restores order. Correct FIFO!</text><text x='10' y='155' class='sub ink'>Stack after: [2(bottom), 3(top)] ready for next dequeue</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing stack with elements 1(bottom),2,3(top). DEQUEUE uses REVERSE to bring oldest element to top, pops it, then REVERSE again to restore order.

given.aim: Determine which queue implementation is feasible using a stack with PUSH, POP, and REVERSE.

terms.meaning: Add element x to the top of the stack.
terms.connects: ENQUEUE can simply call PUSH(x) — one instruction, O(1).

terms.meaning: Reverses all elements in the stack in one instruction (single logical operation).
terms.example: Stack [a,b,c] (c on top) -> REVERSE -> [c,b,a] (a on top).
terms.connects: Enables DEQUEUE to access the bottom element using: REVERSE, POP, REVERSE.

terms.meaning: The first element enqueued must be the first dequeued.
terms.example: Enqueue 1,2,3; dequeue returns 1,2,3 in that order.
terms.connects: We need to reach the bottom of the stack to dequeue the oldest element.

given.plan: Try option B: ENQUEUE = PUSH(x) (1 instruction). DEQUEUE = REVERSE, POP, REVERSE (3 instructions, but a fixed/constant number, which counts as 'a sequence'). This correctly returns the bottom element. Options C and D are less natural; option A is wrong since a queue CAN be implemented.

steps.title: Design ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE using the modified stack
steps.formula_raw:
ENQUEUE(x): PUSH(x)
DEQUEUE: REVERSE; POP; REVERSE
steps.apply: ENQUEUE is a single PUSH operation. DEQUEUE reverses the stack (bringing the oldest/bottom element to top), pops it (removes the oldest element, satisfying FIFO), then reverses again to restore the remaining elements in their original relative order.
steps.note: This matches option B exactly: ENQUEUE takes a single instruction, DEQUEUE takes a sequence (3 instructions).

steps.title: Verify FIFO correctness and eliminate other options
steps.apply: Enqueue 1, 2, 3: stack = [1(bottom), 2, 3(top)]. DEQUEUE: REVERSE -> [3,2,1(top)], POP -> returns 1, stack=[3,2(top)], REVERSE -> [2(bottom),3(top)]. Next DEQUEUE: REVERSE->[3,2(top)], POP->2, REVERSE->[3(top)]. Correct FIFO order 1,2,3. Option A is false (queue can be implemented). Option C (ENQUEUE multi, DEQUEUE single) is not possible because a single POP always gets the most recently pushed element (LIFO). Option D requires both single-instruction which is impossible with standard stack ops alone.
steps.note: Only option B correctly implements a queue with the given modified stack.

solution.result: A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a single instruction (PUSH) and DEQUEUE takes a sequence of instructions (REVERSE, POP, REVERSE).

questions.formula_note: Queue via stack with REVERSE: ENQUEUE = PUSH(x); DEQUEUE = REVERSE + POP + REVERSE. Simulates FIFO using LIFO + reversal.

questions.to_find: The value printed by the C program

understand.plain: The function stkFunc implements a simple stack using a global array A. opcode -1 sets the stack size, opcode 0 pushes a value, and any other opcode pops a value. The program pushes 5, 10, 3 and then pops twice, adding the two popped values.

keywords.explain: Controls stack operation: -1 = init/set size, 0 = push, other = pop.
keywords.example: stkFunc(0, 5) pushes 5; stkFunc(1, 0) pops the top element.

keywords.explain: The local stkTop inside stkFunc is static and shadows the global stkTop. It retains its value between calls.
keywords.example: After stkFunc(0,5), local stkTop=1; after stkFunc(0,10), local stkTop=2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 170'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='400' height='170' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='22' class='lbl ink'>Stack state after pushes: stkTop=3</text><rect x='10' y='35' width='60' height='35' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='58' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>5</text><rect x='75' y='35' width='60' height='35' class='hi'/><text x='105' y='58' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>10</text><rect x='140' y='35' width='60' height='35' class='ok'/><text x='170' y='58' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>3</text><text x='140' y='90' class='sub ink'>^ top (stkTop=3)</text><text x='10' y='115' class='sub ink'>1st stkFunc(1,0): --stkTop=2, returns A[2]=3</text><text x='10' y='135' class='sub ink'>2nd stkFunc(1,0): --stkTop=1, returns A[1]=10</text><text x='10' y='158' class='lbl ink'>printf: 3 + 10 = 13</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Stack with elements B[0]=5, B[1]=10, B[2]=3 (top). First pop returns 3, second pop returns 10. Sum = 13.

given.aim: Trace the C program to find the value printed by printf.

terms.meaning: Initializes the stack: sets static local size=20.
terms.example: After this call, the stack can hold up to 20 elements.

terms.meaning: Pushes value x onto the stack (A[stkTop++] = x).
terms.example: stkFunc(0,5): A[0]=5, stkTop=1; stkFunc(0,10): A[1]=10, stkTop=2; stkFunc(0,3): A[2]=3, stkTop=3.
terms.connects: After three pushes, stack = [5, 10, 3] with stkTop=3.

terms.meaning: Pops the top element (opcode != -1 and != 0, so default case: return A[--stkTop]).
terms.example: First call: stkTop goes from 3 to 2, returns A[2]=3. Second call: stkTop goes from 2 to 1, returns A[1]=10.
terms.connects: The two return values 3 and 10 are added in the printf expression.

given.plan: Trace each stkFunc call in sequence, tracking the static stkTop variable and the array A. Note: in printf, the order of evaluation of arguments is unspecified in C, but both stkFunc(1,0) calls are in the same expression '+', and the two values are 3 and 10 regardless of order. Sum = 13.

steps.title: Trace the push operations
steps.apply: stkFunc(-1,20): size=20, stkTop=0. stkFunc(0,5): A[0]=5, stkTop=1. stkFunc(0,10): A[1]=10, stkTop=2. stkFunc(0,3): A[2]=3, stkTop=3. Stack contents: A[0]=5, A[1]=10, A[2]=3.
steps.note: The static local stkTop (inside stkFunc) is used, not the global stkTop. The two are different variables.

steps.title: Trace the pop operations and compute the sum
steps.apply: First stkFunc(1,0): stkTop=3 > 0, so return A[--stkTop] = A[2] = 3, stkTop is now 2. Second stkFunc(1,0): stkTop=2 > 0, so return A[--stkTop] = A[1] = 10, stkTop is now 1. printf prints 3 + 10 = 13.
steps.note: Even though C does not guarantee evaluation order of function arguments in a sum expression, both pops return 3 and 10 regardless of order, so the sum is always 13.

questions.formula_note: Stack simulation in C using a static array and opcode dispatch. Key insight: static local variable stkTop persists across calls and shadows the global stkTop.
